titleHtml: Unit 2: Chemical processes require energy change as atoms are rearranged
title: Unit 2: Chemical processes require energy change as atoms are rearranged

chapters.titleHtml: Chapter 2.1: How are chemical processes part of our lives?
chapters.title: Chapter 2.1: How are chemical processes part of our lives?

concepts.titleHtml: 2.1 Concept 1: Applications of chemistry are everywhere in the world around you — p.104–105
concepts.title: 2.1 Concept 1: Applications of chemistry are everywhere in the world around you — p.104–105

kps.title: KP1: Chemical reactions
kps.coreHtml: A chemical reaction is a process in which atoms of one or more substances are rearranged to form different substances.
kps.core: A chemical reaction is a process in which atoms of one or more substances are rearranged to form different substances.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify chemical reactions in visible events such as fireworks and in less obvious everyday processes.
kps.application: Identify chemical reactions in visible events such as fireworks and in less obvious everyday processes.

kps.title: KP2: Chemistry in living systems
kps.coreHtml: Chemical reactions help maintain health and sustain life.
kps.core: Chemical reactions help maintain health and sustain life.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect chemistry to food digestion, nutrients, body cells, and living processes.
kps.application: Connect chemistry to food digestion, nutrients, body cells, and living processes.

kps.title: KP3: Chemistry in medicine
kps.coreHtml: Chemistry supports medical tools and technologies that help visualize or understand the body.
kps.core: Chemistry supports medical tools and technologies that help visualize or understand the body.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how chemical knowledge can contribute to medical advances such as imaging technologies.
kps.application: Explain how chemical knowledge can contribute to medical advances such as imaging technologies.

kps.titleHtml: KP4: Chemistry in personal and consumer products
kps.title: KP4: Chemistry in personal and consumer products
kps.coreHtml: Personal care products and many consumer materials are designed with specific chemical properties.
kps.core: Personal care products and many consumer materials are designed with specific chemical properties.
kps.applicationHtml: Analyze how chemistry affects the production, use, and safety of everyday products.
kps.application: Analyze how chemistry affects the production, use, and safety of everyday products.

kps.titleHtml: KP5: Chemistry in transportation and pollution
kps.title: KP5: Chemistry in transportation and pollution
kps.coreHtml: Transportation technologies depend on chemistry but can also contribute to air, land, and water pollution.
kps.core: Transportation technologies depend on chemistry but can also contribute to air, land, and water pollution.
kps.applicationHtml: Evaluate both positive and negative effects of chemistry in transportation.
kps.application: Evaluate both positive and negative effects of chemistry in transportation.

kps.title: KP6: Synthetic materials
kps.coreHtml: Synthetic materials such as plastics and synthetic fibres are human-made products with designed properties.
kps.core: Synthetic materials such as plastics and synthetic fibres are human-made products with designed properties.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare the advantages of durability and usefulness with disadvantages such as persistence and pollution.
kps.application: Compare the advantages of durability and usefulness with disadvantages such as persistence and pollution.

concepts.titleHtml: 2.1 Concept 2: Knowing how to handle chemicals helps keep us and our environment safe — p.108–110
concepts.title: 2.1 Concept 2: Knowing how to handle chemicals helps keep us and our environment safe — p.108–110

kps.title: KP1: Chemical hazards
kps.coreHtml: Some chemical compounds have properties that make them potentially dangerous.
kps.core: Some chemical compounds have properties that make them potentially dangerous.
kps.applicationHtml: Recognize that safe handling depends on understanding the chemical’s risks.
kps.application: Recognize that safe handling depends on understanding the chemical’s risks.

kps.title: KP2: WHMIS labels
kps.coreHtml: WHMIS labels provide information about chemical hazards in laboratories and workplaces.
kps.core: WHMIS labels provide information about chemical hazards in laboratories and workplaces.
kps.applicationHtml: Use WHMIS symbols to identify risks such as flammability, corrosion, toxicity, or pressure hazards.
kps.application: Use WHMIS symbols to identify risks such as flammability, corrosion, toxicity, or pressure hazards.

kps.title: KP3: Safety Data Sheets
kps.coreHtml: Safety Data Sheets, or SDS, provide information about chemical properties, handling, storage, disposal, hazards, and first aid.
kps.core: Safety Data Sheets, or SDS, provide information about chemical properties, handling, storage, disposal, hazards, and first aid.
kps.applicationHtml: Consult SDS information before planning or carrying out chemical work.
kps.application: Consult SDS information before planning or carrying out chemical work.

kps.title: KP4: Protective equipment and behaviour
kps.coreHtml: Safe chemical work requires protective equipment and responsible behaviour.
kps.core: Safe chemical work requires protective equipment and responsible behaviour.
kps.applicationHtml: Use safety goggles, lab coats or aprons, tied-back hair, and no eating or drinking during investigations.
kps.application: Use safety goggles, lab coats or aprons, tied-back hair, and no eating or drinking during investigations.

kps.titleHtml: KP5: Emergency response in the classroom
kps.title: KP5: Emergency response in the classroom
kps.coreHtml: Chemical safety includes knowing emergency equipment, exits, eyewash stations, and spill-response procedures.
kps.core: Chemical safety includes knowing emergency equipment, exits, eyewash stations, and spill-response procedures.
kps.applicationHtml: Respond appropriately to spills, eye exposure, skin contact, and other classroom hazards.
kps.application: Respond appropriately to spills, eye exposure, skin contact, and other classroom hazards.

kps.titleHtml: KP6: Heating, fumes, and handling containers
kps.title: KP6: Heating, fumes, and handling containers
kps.coreHtml: Heating chemicals and handling fumes require extra caution.
kps.core: Heating chemicals and handling fumes require extra caution.
kps.applicationHtml: Point heated containers away from people, never leave flames unattended, and waft fumes instead of smelling directly.
kps.application: Point heated containers away from people, never leave flames unattended, and waft fumes instead of smelling directly.

kps.titleHtml: KP7: Minimizing personal and environmental risk
kps.title: KP7: Minimizing personal and environmental risk
kps.coreHtml: Chemical safety includes reducing risks to people and reducing harm to the environment.
kps.core: Chemical safety includes reducing risks to people and reducing harm to the environment.
kps.applicationHtml: Choose handling, storage, and disposal methods that protect both personal safety and environmental quality.
kps.application: Choose handling, storage, and disposal methods that protect both personal safety and environmental quality.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Take Action — Chemicals in Our Water — p.106–107
concepts.title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Take Action — Chemicals in Our Water — p.106–107

kps.title: KP1: Water as a solvent
kps.coreHtml: Water is called the universal solvent because many substances can dissolve in it.
kps.core: Water is called the universal solvent because many substances can dissolve in it.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why water can contain both naturally occurring substances and pollutants.
kps.application: Explain why water can contain both naturally occurring substances and pollutants.

kps.title: KP2: Water pollution sources
kps.coreHtml: Water pollution can come from household, mining, industrial, pharmaceutical, and plastic waste.
kps.core: Water pollution can come from household, mining, industrial, pharmaceutical, and plastic waste.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify human activities that introduce harmful substances into water systems.
kps.application: Identify human activities that introduce harmful substances into water systems.

kps.title: KP3: Wastewater and stormwater
kps.coreHtml: Wastewater treatment can reduce but not remove all pollutants; stormwater can flow directly into local waterways.
kps.core: Wastewater treatment can reduce but not remove all pollutants; stormwater can flow directly into local waterways.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare treated wastewater with untreated storm drain runoff.
kps.application: Compare treated wastewater with untreated storm drain runoff.

kps.titleHtml: KP4: Safe drinking water and community responsibility
kps.title: KP4: Safe drinking water and community responsibility
kps.coreHtml: Water quality issues affect communities differently, including some First Nations communities with unsafe drinking water.
kps.core: Water quality issues affect communities differently, including some First Nations communities with unsafe drinking water.
kps.applicationHtml: Develop actions that improve awareness, reduce pollution, or support cleaner water systems.
kps.application: Develop actions that improve awareness, reduce pollution, or support cleaner water systems.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — At Issue: Fighting Fires with Chemistry — p.112–113
concepts.title: Optional Extension — At Issue: Fighting Fires with Chemistry — p.112–113

kps.title: KP1: Fire extinguisher types
kps.coreHtml: Different classes of fires require different types of fire extinguishers.
kps.core: Different classes of fires require different types of fire extinguishers.
kps.applicationHtml: Match extinguisher type to fuel source, such as ordinary combustibles, flammable liquids, electrical equipment, metals, or cooking oils.
kps.application: Match extinguisher type to fuel source, such as ordinary combustibles, flammable liquids, electrical equipment, metals, or cooking oils.

kps.title: KP2: Correct extinguisher choice
kps.coreHtml: The wrong extinguisher can spread a fire or create new hazards.
kps.core: The wrong extinguisher can spread a fire or create new hazards.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why water should not be used on electrical or oil-based fires.
kps.application: Explain why water should not be used on electrical or oil-based fires.

kps.title: KP3: Fire extinguisher labels
kps.coreHtml: Fire extinguishers use letters and symbols to show which fire classes they can safely address.
kps.core: Fire extinguishers use letters and symbols to show which fire classes they can safely address.
kps.applicationHtml: Read extinguisher labels before deciding how to respond to a fire.
kps.application: Read extinguisher labels before deciding how to respond to a fire.

kps.titleHtml: KP4: Chemical trade-offs in fire safety
kps.title: KP4: Chemical trade-offs in fire safety
kps.coreHtml: Fire-suppression chemicals can have benefits and environmental or health risks.
kps.core: Fire-suppression chemicals can have benefits and environmental or health risks.
kps.applicationHtml: Evaluate why some chemicals, such as halons, may be phased out or require careful disposal.
kps.application: Evaluate why some chemicals, such as halons, may be phased out or require careful disposal.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-A: Is It Worth It? — p.114–115
concepts.title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-A: Is It Worth It? — p.114–115

kps.title: KP1: Product life cycle
kps.coreHtml: A product’s life cycle includes raw material extraction, processing, manufacture, distribution, use, recycling, and disposal.
kps.core: A product’s life cycle includes raw material extraction, processing, manufacture, distribution, use, recycling, and disposal.
kps.applicationHtml: Analyze the environmental and social impact of a synthetic product across its life cycle.
kps.application: Analyze the environmental and social impact of a synthetic product across its life cycle.

kps.titleHtml: KP2: Positive and negative product impacts
kps.title: KP2: Positive and negative product impacts
kps.coreHtml: Synthetic products can benefit society while also producing unintended environmental consequences.
kps.core: Synthetic products can benefit society while also producing unintended environmental consequences.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare usefulness, durability, cost, waste, toxicity, and pollution for one product.
kps.application: Compare usefulness, durability, cost, waste, toxicity, and pollution for one product.

kps.title: KP3: Green alternatives and recycling
kps.coreHtml: Negative impacts can sometimes be reduced through recycling programs, reuse, or greener alternatives.
kps.core: Negative impacts can sometimes be reduced through recycling programs, reuse, or greener alternatives.
kps.applicationHtml: Evaluate whether a synthetic product is “worth it” based on evidence.
kps.application: Evaluate whether a synthetic product is “worth it” based on evidence.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-B: Prepare an Emergency Response Plan — p.116–117
concepts.title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-B: Prepare an Emergency Response Plan — p.116–117

kps.title: KP1: Emergency preparedness
kps.coreHtml: Chemical accidents can threaten people and the environment, so planning reduces harm.
kps.core: Chemical accidents can threaten people and the environment, so planning reduces harm.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify possible emergency scenarios involving gas leaks, fires, spills, or evacuation needs.
kps.application: Identify possible emergency scenarios involving gas leaks, fires, spills, or evacuation needs.

kps.title: KP2: Response plan components
kps.coreHtml: An emergency response plan should include preparation, evacuation procedures, communication, cleanup, and disposal.
kps.core: An emergency response plan should include preparation, evacuation procedures, communication, cleanup, and disposal.
kps.applicationHtml: Build a plan that explains who acts, where people go, and how hazards are managed.
kps.application: Build a plan that explains who acts, where people go, and how hazards are managed.

kps.title: KP3: Communication and audience
kps.coreHtml: Emergency plans must be communicated clearly to the people who need to follow them.
kps.core: Emergency plans must be communicated clearly to the people who need to follow them.
kps.applicationHtml: Present emergency information in a format appropriate for a school, workplace, or community.
kps.application: Present emergency information in a format appropriate for a school, workplace, or community.

chapters.titleHtml: Chapter 2.2: What happens to atoms in a chemical reaction?
chapters.title: Chapter 2.2: What happens to atoms in a chemical reaction?

concepts.titleHtml: 2.2 Concept 1: Atoms bond together to form ionic and covalent compounds — p.120–122
concepts.title: 2.2 Concept 1: Atoms bond together to form ionic and covalent compounds — p.120–122

kps.title: KP1: Compounds
kps.coreHtml: Compounds are made of atoms of different elements chemically combined in specific proportions.
kps.core: Compounds are made of atoms of different elements chemically combined in specific proportions.
kps.applicationHtml: Classify substances as elements or compounds before analyzing a chemical reaction.
kps.application: Classify substances as elements or compounds before analyzing a chemical reaction.

kps.title: KP2: Ionic compounds
kps.coreHtml: Ionic compounds are made of positively charged cations and negatively charged anions.
kps.core: Ionic compounds are made of positively charged cations and negatively charged anions.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify ionic compounds as substances formed from oppositely charged ions.
kps.application: Identify ionic compounds as substances formed from oppositely charged ions.

kps.title: KP3: Ionic bonds
kps.coreHtml: An ionic bond is a strong attraction between oppositely charged ions.
kps.core: An ionic bond is a strong attraction between oppositely charged ions.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how electron transfer between metal and non-metal atoms forms stable ions.
kps.application: Explain how electron transfer between metal and non-metal atoms forms stable ions.

kps.title: KP4: Covalent compounds
kps.coreHtml: Covalent compounds form when atoms of non-metal elements share electrons.
kps.core: Covalent compounds form when atoms of non-metal elements share electrons.
kps.applicationHtml: Distinguish covalent bonding from ionic bonding.
kps.application: Distinguish covalent bonding from ionic bonding.

kps.title: KP5: Covalent bonds
kps.coreHtml: A covalent bond is a strong attraction formed when atoms share valence electrons.
kps.core: A covalent bond is a strong attraction formed when atoms share valence electrons.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify single, double, and triple covalent bonds in simple molecules.
kps.application: Identify single, double, and triple covalent bonds in simple molecules.

kps.title: KP6: Molecules
kps.coreHtml: A molecule is the smallest independent unit of a covalent compound or a covalently bonded element.
kps.core: A molecule is the smallest independent unit of a covalent compound or a covalently bonded element.
kps.applicationHtml: Distinguish molecular substances from ionic crystal lattices.
kps.application: Distinguish molecular substances from ionic crystal lattices.

kps.title: KP7: Formula units versus molecules
kps.coreHtml: Ionic compounds are represented by formula units, while most covalent compounds exist as molecules.
kps.core: Ionic compounds are represented by formula units, while most covalent compounds exist as molecules.
kps.applicationHtml: Avoid describing ionic compounds such as sodium chloride as separate molecules.
kps.application: Avoid describing ionic compounds such as sodium chloride as separate molecules.

concepts.titleHtml: 2.2 Concept 2: Bonds are broken, atoms are rearranged, and new bonds are formed — p.123–124
concepts.title: 2.2 Concept 2: Bonds are broken, atoms are rearranged, and new bonds are formed — p.123–124

kps.title: KP1: Atom rearrangement
kps.coreHtml: In a chemical reaction, atoms are rearranged to form new substances.
kps.core: In a chemical reaction, atoms are rearranged to form new substances.
kps.applicationHtml: Track atoms from reactants to products.
kps.application: Track atoms from reactants to products.

kps.title: KP2: Breaking chemical bonds
kps.coreHtml: Existing chemical bonds must be broken before atoms can rearrange.
kps.core: Existing chemical bonds must be broken before atoms can rearrange.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why energy is needed to separate bonded atoms or ions.
kps.application: Explain why energy is needed to separate bonded atoms or ions.

kps.title: KP3: Forming new chemical bonds
kps.coreHtml: New bonds form between rearranged atoms to produce new substances.
kps.core: New bonds form between rearranged atoms to produce new substances.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify product formation as evidence that new bonds have formed.
kps.application: Identify product formation as evidence that new bonds have formed.

kps.title: KP4: Bond energy changes
kps.coreHtml: Breaking bonds requires energy; forming bonds releases energy.
kps.core: Breaking bonds requires energy; forming bonds releases energy.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect chemical reactions to energy changes.
kps.application: Connect chemical reactions to energy changes.

kps.title: KP5: Stability and valence shells
kps.coreHtml: Bond formation can make atoms more stable by filling valence shells.
kps.core: Bond formation can make atoms more stable by filling valence shells.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why atoms form ionic or covalent bonds.
kps.application: Explain why atoms form ionic or covalent bonds.

concepts.titleHtml: 2.2 Concept 3: Mass cannot be created or destroyed in a chemical reaction — p.125–126
concepts.title: 2.2 Concept 3: Mass cannot be created or destroyed in a chemical reaction — p.125–126

kps.titleHtml: KP1: Law of conservation of mass
kps.title: KP1: Law of conservation of mass
kps.coreHtml: In a chemical reaction, the total mass of substances used equals the total mass of substances produced.
kps.core: In a chemical reaction, the total mass of substances used equals the total mass of substances produced.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why atoms are conserved during a chemical reaction.
kps.application: Explain why atoms are conserved during a chemical reaction.

kps.title: KP2: Closed systems
kps.coreHtml: In a closed system, energy can enter or leave, but matter cannot.
kps.core: In a closed system, energy can enter or leave, but matter cannot.
kps.applicationHtml: Use closed systems to test whether mass is conserved.
kps.application: Use closed systems to test whether mass is conserved.

kps.title: KP3: Open systems
kps.coreHtml: In an open system, both matter and energy can enter or leave.
kps.core: In an open system, both matter and energy can enter or leave.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why gases escaping from a reaction can make mass measurements appear to change.
kps.application: Explain why gases escaping from a reaction can make mass measurements appear to change.

kps.title: KP4: Lavoisier’s contribution
kps.coreHtml: Careful mass measurements before and after reactions helped establish the law of conservation of mass.
kps.core: Careful mass measurements before and after reactions helped establish the law of conservation of mass.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect experimental evidence to scientific laws.
kps.application: Connect experimental evidence to scientific laws.

kps.title: KP5: Atom conservation
kps.coreHtml: Atoms are not created or destroyed in chemical reactions; they are rearranged.
kps.core: Atoms are not created or destroyed in chemical reactions; they are rearranged.
kps.applicationHtml: Use atom counting to check whether a chemical equation obeys conservation of mass.
kps.application: Use atom counting to check whether a chemical equation obeys conservation of mass.

concepts.titleHtml: 2.2 Concept 4: A chemical equation represents what happens to the atoms in a reaction — p.128–133
concepts.title: 2.2 Concept 4: A chemical equation represents what happens to the atoms in a reaction — p.128–133

kps.title: KP1: Chemical equations
kps.coreHtml: A chemical equation uses words or chemical formulas to represent a chemical reaction.
kps.core: A chemical equation uses words or chemical formulas to represent a chemical reaction.
kps.applicationHtml: Interpret the arrow as “produces” or “yields” and plus signs as “reacts with” or “and.”
kps.application: Interpret the arrow as “produces” or “yields” and plus signs as “reacts with” or “and.”

kps.title: KP2: Reactants and products
kps.coreHtml: Reactants are substances that undergo chemical change; products are substances formed in the reaction.
kps.core: Reactants are substances that undergo chemical change; products are substances formed in the reaction.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify reactants on the left side and products on the right side of a chemical equation.
kps.application: Identify reactants on the left side and products on the right side of a chemical equation.

kps.title: KP3: Word equations
kps.coreHtml: A word equation describes reactants and products using names.
kps.core: A word equation describes reactants and products using names.
kps.applicationHtml: Translate a reaction description into a word equation before using formulas.
kps.application: Translate a reaction description into a word equation before using formulas.

kps.title: KP4: Skeleton equations
kps.coreHtml: A skeleton equation uses chemical formulas but may not show correct reactant-product proportions.
kps.core: A skeleton equation uses chemical formulas but may not show correct reactant-product proportions.
kps.applicationHtml: Recognize that a skeleton equation may not obey conservation of mass.
kps.application: Recognize that a skeleton equation may not obey conservation of mass.

kps.title: KP5: Balanced chemical equations
kps.coreHtml: A balanced chemical equation has the same number of atoms of each element on both sides.
kps.core: A balanced chemical equation has the same number of atoms of each element on both sides.
kps.applicationHtml: Use conservation of mass to check whether an equation is balanced.
kps.application: Use conservation of mass to check whether an equation is balanced.

kps.title: KP6: Coefficients
kps.coreHtml: A coefficient is placed in front of a chemical formula to show the ratio of substances in a balanced equation.
kps.core: A coefficient is placed in front of a chemical formula to show the ratio of substances in a balanced equation.
kps.applicationHtml: Balance equations by changing coefficients, not subscripts.
kps.application: Balance equations by changing coefficients, not subscripts.

kps.title: KP7: Subscripts
kps.coreHtml: A subscript in a chemical formula shows how many atoms of an element are present in one molecule or formula unit.
kps.core: A subscript in a chemical formula shows how many atoms of an element are present in one molecule or formula unit.
kps.applicationHtml: Avoid changing subscripts because this changes the identity of the substance.
kps.application: Avoid changing subscripts because this changes the identity of the substance.

kps.title: KP8: States of matter
kps.coreHtml: Chemical equations may include state symbols such as \((s)\), \((l)\), \((g)\), and \((aq)\).
kps.core: Chemical equations may include state symbols such as \((s)\), \((l)\), \((g)\), and \((aq)\).
kps.applicationHtml: Include physical states when describing reactants and products.
kps.application: Include physical states when describing reactants and products.

kps.title: KP9: Diatomic and polyatomic elements
kps.coreHtml: Some elements naturally exist as molecules such as \(H_2\), \(N_2\), \(O_2\), \(F_2\), \(Cl_2\), \(Br_2\), \(I_2\), \(S_8\), and \(P_4\).
kps.core: Some elements naturally exist as molecules such as \(H 2\), \(N 2\), \(O 2\), \(F 2\), \(Cl 2\), \(Br 2\), \(I 2\), \(S 8\), and \(P 4\).
kps.applicationHtml: Write correct formulas for elemental reactants or products.
kps.application: Write correct formulas for elemental reactants or products.

kps.title: KP10: Balancing strategy
kps.coreHtml: Balance compounds first, elements last, and treat unchanged polyatomic ions as single units when possible.
kps.core: Balance compounds first, elements last, and treat unchanged polyatomic ions as single units when possible.
kps.applicationHtml: Check final atom counts on both sides of the equation.
kps.application: Check final atom counts on both sides of the equation.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Take a Stand — What does the law of conservation of mass tell us about waste? — p.127
concepts.title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Take a Stand — What does the law of conservation of mass tell us about waste? — p.127

kps.title: KP1: Matter and waste
kps.coreHtml: The law of conservation of mass means waste matter does not disappear when it is buried, dumped, burned, or released.
kps.core: The law of conservation of mass means waste matter does not disappear when it is buried, dumped, burned, or released.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect chemical waste to long-term environmental responsibility.
kps.application: Connect chemical waste to long-term environmental responsibility.

kps.title: KP2: Reducing versus recycling
kps.coreHtml: Reducing waste prevents material use, while recycling changes or reuses material already produced.
kps.core: Reducing waste prevents material use, while recycling changes or reuses material already produced.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why reducing waste is different from managing waste after it exists.
kps.application: Explain why reducing waste is different from managing waste after it exists.

kps.title: KP3: Waste classification
kps.coreHtml: Waste can be classified by danger level or source, such as hazardous, residential, municipal, agricultural, or industrial.
kps.core: Waste can be classified by danger level or source, such as hazardous, residential, municipal, agricultural, or industrial.
kps.applicationHtml: Evaluate how classification helps or limits environmental decision-making.
kps.application: Evaluate how classification helps or limits environmental decision-making.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — At Issue: Can traditional methods be used to tan leather sustainably? — p.134
concepts.title: Optional Extension — At Issue: Can traditional methods be used to tan leather sustainably? — p.134

kps.title: KP1: Tanning as chemical processing
kps.coreHtml: Treating raw hide involves chemical processes that prevent decay and keep leather flexible and waterproof.
kps.core: Treating raw hide involves chemical processes that prevent decay and keep leather flexible and waterproof.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect traditional tanning knowledge to chemical change.
kps.application: Connect traditional tanning knowledge to chemical change.

kps.title: KP2: Brain tanning
kps.coreHtml: Brain tanning uses fats and oils from brain tissue to change the chemical properties of hide.
kps.core: Brain tanning uses fats and oils from brain tissue to change the chemical properties of hide.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare natural tanning processes with industrial chemical methods.
kps.application: Compare natural tanning processes with industrial chemical methods.

kps.title: KP3: Sustainability trade-offs
kps.coreHtml: Traditional tanning can use natural materials and more of the whole animal, while industrial tanning may use toxic chemicals such as chromium salts.
kps.core: Traditional tanning can use natural materials and more of the whole animal, while industrial tanning may use toxic chemicals such as chromium salts.
kps.applicationHtml: Evaluate tanning methods by sustainability, safety, scale, and environmental impact.
kps.application: Evaluate tanning methods by sustainability, safety, scale, and environmental impact.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-C: The Law of Conservation of Mass — p.136–137
concepts.title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-C: The Law of Conservation of Mass — p.136–137

kps.title: KP1: Testing conservation of mass
kps.coreHtml: Conservation of mass can be investigated by measuring reactant and product systems before and after reaction.
kps.core: Conservation of mass can be investigated by measuring reactant and product systems before and after reaction.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare mass changes in closed and open systems.
kps.application: Compare mass changes in closed and open systems.

kps.title: KP2: Closed-system reaction
kps.coreHtml: A sealed container prevents matter from escaping during a reaction.
kps.core: A sealed container prevents matter from escaping during a reaction.
kps.applicationHtml: Use a closed system to show that total mass remains constant.
kps.application: Use a closed system to show that total mass remains constant.

kps.title: KP3: Open-system reaction
kps.coreHtml: An open container allows matter such as gas to leave the system.
kps.core: An open container allows matter such as gas to leave the system.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why measured mass can decrease when gas escapes.
kps.application: Explain why measured mass can decrease when gas escapes.

kps.title: KP4: Evidence of chemical reaction
kps.coreHtml: Observations such as colour change, precipitate formation, gas production, or temperature change can indicate a chemical reaction.
kps.core: Observations such as colour change, precipitate formation, gas production, or temperature change can indicate a chemical reaction.
kps.applicationHtml: Record observations and connect them to reaction evidence.
kps.application: Record observations and connect them to reaction evidence.

chapters.titleHtml: Chapter 2.3: How is energy involved in chemical processes?
chapters.title: Chapter 2.3: How is energy involved in chemical processes?

concepts.titleHtml: 2.3 Concept 1: Matter and energy interact in physical and chemical changes — p.140
concepts.title: 2.3 Concept 1: Matter and energy interact in physical and chemical changes — p.140

kps.title: KP1: Matter and energy interaction
kps.coreHtml: Matter and energy continually interact in physical and chemical changes.
kps.core: Matter and energy continually interact in physical and chemical changes.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify energy transfers in everyday processes such as heating water, cooking food, or melting ice.
kps.application: Identify energy transfers in everyday processes such as heating water, cooking food, or melting ice.

kps.title: KP2: Energy input for change
kps.coreHtml: Many changes require an input of energy to begin or continue.
kps.core: Many changes require an input of energy to begin or continue.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why boiling water, toasting bread, or igniting propane requires added energy.
kps.application: Explain why boiling water, toasting bread, or igniting propane requires added energy.

kps.title: KP3: Activation energy
kps.coreHtml: Activation energy is the minimum energy needed for a reaction to occur.
kps.core: Activation energy is the minimum energy needed for a reaction to occur.
kps.applicationHtml: Describe activation energy as a barrier or “hill” that reactants must overcome.
kps.application: Describe activation energy as a barrier or “hill” that reactants must overcome.

kps.title: KP4: Reactant collisions
kps.coreHtml: Reactants must collide with enough energy to begin breaking bonds.
kps.core: Reactants must collide with enough energy to begin breaking bonds.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect successful collisions to the start of a chemical reaction.
kps.application: Connect successful collisions to the start of a chemical reaction.

kps.titleHtml: KP5: Initial energy and self-sustaining reactions
kps.title: KP5: Initial energy and self-sustaining reactions
kps.coreHtml: Some reactions need an initial energy input, then continue because the reaction releases enough energy to keep going.
kps.core: Some reactions need an initial energy input, then continue because the reaction releases enough energy to keep going.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why a propane torch needs a spark to start but can keep burning after ignition.
kps.application: Explain why a propane torch needs a spark to start but can keep burning after ignition.

kps.title: KP6: Energy in physical changes
kps.coreHtml: Physical changes can involve energy transfer even when no new substance forms.
kps.core: Physical changes can involve energy transfer even when no new substance forms.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain melting ice as a process involving energy moving from warmer surroundings to the ice.
kps.application: Explain melting ice as a process involving energy moving from warmer surroundings to the ice.

concepts.titleHtml: 2.3 Concept 2: Energy is transferred between chemical reactions and the surroundings — p.142–145
concepts.title: 2.3 Concept 2: Energy is transferred between chemical reactions and the surroundings — p.142–145

kps.titleHtml: KP1: Energy changes in chemical reactions
kps.title: KP1: Energy changes in chemical reactions
kps.coreHtml: All chemical reactions are accompanied by changes in energy.
kps.core: All chemical reactions are accompanied by changes in energy.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect photosynthesis and cellular respiration to energy stored or released through chemical reactions.
kps.application: Connect photosynthesis and cellular respiration to energy stored or released through chemical reactions.

kps.title: KP2: System and surroundings
kps.coreHtml: The system is the reacting materials; the surroundings include everything outside the system.
kps.core: The system is the reacting materials; the surroundings include everything outside the system.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify the reaction mixture as the system and the container, air, classroom, or observer as surroundings.
kps.application: Identify the reaction mixture as the system and the container, air, classroom, or observer as surroundings.

kps.title: KP3: Conservation of energy
kps.coreHtml: Energy cannot be created or destroyed; energy leaving the system enters the surroundings, and energy entering the system comes from the surroundings.
kps.core: Energy cannot be created or destroyed; energy leaving the system enters the surroundings, and energy entering the system comes from the surroundings.
kps.applicationHtml: Trace energy transfer during exothermic and endothermic reactions.
kps.application: Trace energy transfer during exothermic and endothermic reactions.

kps.title: KP4: Exothermic reactions
kps.coreHtml: An exothermic reaction has a net release of energy to the surroundings.
kps.core: An exothermic reaction has a net release of energy to the surroundings.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify reactions where the surroundings warm up or light/heat is released.
kps.application: Identify reactions where the surroundings warm up or light/heat is released.

kps.title: KP5: Endothermic reactions
kps.coreHtml: An endothermic reaction has a net absorption of energy from the surroundings.
kps.core: An endothermic reaction has a net absorption of energy from the surroundings.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify reactions where the surroundings cool down or energy is absorbed.
kps.application: Identify reactions where the surroundings cool down or energy is absorbed.

kps.title: KP6: Bond-breaking and bond-forming energy
kps.coreHtml: Breaking bonds absorbs energy; forming bonds releases energy.
kps.core: Breaking bonds absorbs energy; forming bonds releases energy.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare total energy absorbed in bond breaking with total energy released in bond forming.
kps.application: Compare total energy absorbed in bond breaking with total energy released in bond forming.

kps.title: KP7: Temperature as evidence
kps.coreHtml: Temperature change can provide evidence for energy transfer in a reaction.
kps.core: Temperature change can provide evidence for energy transfer in a reaction.
kps.applicationHtml: Use increasing temperature as evidence for exothermic change and decreasing temperature as evidence for endothermic change.
kps.application: Use increasing temperature as evidence for exothermic change and decreasing temperature as evidence for endothermic change.

kps.title: KP8: Energy-level diagrams
kps.coreHtml: Energy-level diagrams show the relative energy of reactants and products and the overall energy change.
kps.core: Energy-level diagrams show the relative energy of reactants and products and the overall energy change.
kps.applicationHtml: Interpret higher-energy products as endothermic and lower-energy products as exothermic.
kps.application: Interpret higher-energy products as endothermic and lower-energy products as exothermic.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — At Issue: How does energy change help to solve crimes? — p.141
concepts.title: Optional Extension — At Issue: How does energy change help to solve crimes? — p.141

kps.title: KP1: Luminol reaction
kps.coreHtml: Luminol can react with iron from hemoglobin to produce a glowing blue-green light.
kps.core: Luminol can react with iron from hemoglobin to produce a glowing blue-green light.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how chemical energy changes can help detect possible blood evidence.
kps.application: Explain how chemical energy changes can help detect possible blood evidence.

kps.title: KP2: Excited electrons and light
kps.coreHtml: Energy absorbed by electrons can raise them to a higher energy level; light is released when they return to a lower energy level.
kps.core: Energy absorbed by electrons can raise them to a higher energy level; light is released when they return to a lower energy level.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect electron energy changes to visible light production.
kps.application: Connect electron energy changes to visible light production.

kps.title: KP3: Limits of chemical evidence
kps.coreHtml: A positive luminol test is not conclusive because other substances can also make luminol emit light.
kps.core: A positive luminol test is not conclusive because other substances can also make luminol emit light.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why further testing, such as DNA testing, may be needed after a luminol result.
kps.application: Explain why further testing, such as DNA testing, may be needed after a luminol result.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — At Issue: Why does it matter how quickly a reaction occurs? — p.146
concepts.title: Optional Extension — At Issue: Why does it matter how quickly a reaction occurs? — p.146

kps.title: KP1: Reaction rate
kps.coreHtml: Every chemical reaction occurs at a certain rate under specific conditions.
kps.core: Every chemical reaction occurs at a certain rate under specific conditions.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why some reactions, such as rusting, are useful when slow, while others must happen quickly.
kps.application: Explain why some reactions, such as rusting, are useful when slow, while others must happen quickly.

kps.title: KP2: Factors affecting reaction rate
kps.coreHtml: Temperature, surface area, concentration or amount of reactant, and catalysts can affect reaction rate.
kps.core: Temperature, surface area, concentration or amount of reactant, and catalysts can affect reaction rate.
kps.applicationHtml: Predict how changing conditions can speed up or slow down a reaction.
kps.application: Predict how changing conditions can speed up or slow down a reaction.

kps.title: KP3: Catalysts
kps.coreHtml: A catalyst speeds up a reaction without being used up.
kps.core: A catalyst speeds up a reaction without being used up.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect catalytic converters and enzymes to faster chemical reactions.
kps.application: Connect catalytic converters and enzymes to faster chemical reactions.

kps.title: KP4: Runaway reactions
kps.coreHtml: Heat released by an exothermic reaction can increase reaction rate and cause an uncontrolled reaction.
kps.core: Heat released by an exothermic reaction can increase reaction rate and cause an uncontrolled reaction.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why industrial reactions need careful heat removal and safety controls.
kps.application: Explain why industrial reactions need careful heat removal and safety controls.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Be Inspired — Ata Sina and the Shape-Shifting Paper — p.148
concepts.title: Optional Extension — Make a Difference: Be Inspired — Ata Sina and the Shape-Shifting Paper — p.148

kps.title: KP1: Thermoplastic polymers and energy
kps.coreHtml: Thermoplastic polymers can absorb energy, increase molecular motion, and change shape.
kps.core: Thermoplastic polymers can absorb energy, increase molecular motion, and change shape.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how energy input can cause shape-shifting paper to fold into three-dimensional structures.
kps.application: Explain how energy input can cause shape-shifting paper to fold into three-dimensional structures.

kps.title: KP2: Chemistry and material design
kps.coreHtml: Chemical properties of materials can be used to design lightweight, strong, and affordable structures.
kps.core: Chemical properties of materials can be used to design lightweight, strong, and affordable structures.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect polymer chemistry to applications such as packaging, art materials, or toys.
kps.application: Connect polymer chemistry to applications such as packaging, art materials, or toys.

kps.title: KP3: Science and creative innovation
kps.coreHtml: Chemistry can combine with art, design, and engineering to create new technologies.
kps.core: Chemistry can combine with art, design, and engineering to create new technologies.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify how personal interests can connect with scientific innovation.
kps.application: Identify how personal interests can connect with scientific innovation.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — Science at Work: Chemistry Connections — p.149
concepts.title: Optional Extension — Science at Work: Chemistry Connections — p.149

kps.title: KP1: Chemistry-related careers
kps.coreHtml: Chemistry knowledge supports careers such as mudlogger, forensic scientist, food scientist, materials technician, laboratory analyst, and fuel researcher.
kps.core: Chemistry knowledge supports careers such as mudlogger, forensic scientist, food scientist, materials technician, laboratory analyst, and fuel researcher.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect chemical reactions and material properties to real workplace tasks.
kps.application: Connect chemical reactions and material properties to real workplace tasks.

kps.title: KP2: Chemistry and public safety
kps.coreHtml: Some chemistry careers involve protecting people, property, and the environment.
kps.core: Some chemistry careers involve protecting people, property, and the environment.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how fire protection engineers or environmental assessors use chemistry knowledge.
kps.application: Explain how fire protection engineers or environmental assessors use chemistry knowledge.

kps.title: KP3: Chemistry and sustainability
kps.coreHtml: Fuel researchers study fuel performance, emissions, renewables, and alternatives.
kps.core: Fuel researchers study fuel performance, emissions, renewables, and alternatives.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect chemical processes to climate and energy decisions.
kps.application: Connect chemical processes to climate and energy decisions.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-D: Exothermic and Endothermic Reactions — p.150–151
concepts.title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-D: Exothermic and Endothermic Reactions — p.150–151

kps.titleHtml: KP1: Temperature data and reaction type
kps.title: KP1: Temperature data and reaction type
kps.coreHtml: Temperature measurements can show whether a reaction releases or absorbs energy overall.
kps.core: Temperature measurements can show whether a reaction releases or absorbs energy overall.
kps.applicationHtml: Classify a reaction as exothermic or endothermic from temperature-change evidence.
kps.application: Classify a reaction as exothermic or endothermic from temperature-change evidence.

kps.title: KP2: Overall energy change
kps.coreHtml: Overall energy change is the net result of energy absorbed to break bonds and energy released when new bonds form.
kps.core: Overall energy change is the net result of energy absorbed to break bonds and energy released when new bonds form.
kps.applicationHtml: Use data and observations to support claims about energy transfer.
kps.application: Use data and observations to support claims about energy transfer.

kps.title: KP3: Reaction evidence
kps.coreHtml: Chemical reactions may show evidence such as temperature change, colour change, or formation of new substances.
kps.core: Chemical reactions may show evidence such as temperature change, colour change, or formation of new substances.
kps.applicationHtml: Record observations and connect them to particle-level changes.
kps.application: Record observations and connect them to particle-level changes.

kps.title: KP4: Particle-level explanation
kps.coreHtml: Exothermic and endothermic classifications can be linked to atoms, ions, bonds broken, and bonds formed.
kps.core: Exothermic and endothermic classifications can be linked to atoms, ions, bonds broken, and bonds formed.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain what happens to atoms and ions during a reaction without relying only on visible changes.
kps.application: Explain what happens to atoms and ions during a reaction without relying only on visible changes.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-E: What Makes the Best Hand Warmer? — p.152
concepts.title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-E: What Makes the Best Hand Warmer? — p.152

kps.title: KP1: Dissolving and energy change
kps.coreHtml: Dissolving ionic compounds in water can be exothermic or endothermic.
kps.core: Dissolving ionic compounds in water can be exothermic or endothermic.
kps.applicationHtml: Test temperature change to decide whether a substance could work as a hand warmer.
kps.application: Test temperature change to decide whether a substance could work as a hand warmer.

kps.title: KP2: Design criteria
kps.coreHtml: A useful hand warmer must balance temperature change, speed, safety, cost, volume, and environmental impact.
kps.core: A useful hand warmer must balance temperature change, speed, safety, cost, volume, and environmental impact.
kps.applicationHtml: Use criteria to compare chemicals instead of choosing only the hottest reaction.
kps.application: Use criteria to compare chemicals instead of choosing only the hottest reaction.

kps.title: KP3: Safe chemical design
kps.coreHtml: Chemical products should be evaluated for both effectiveness and safety.
kps.core: Chemical products should be evaluated for both effectiveness and safety.
kps.applicationHtml: Include skin safety, handling safety, cleanup, and disposal in product design.
kps.application: Include skin safety, handling safety, cleanup, and disposal in product design.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-F: Chemical Reactions as Energy Sources — p.153
concepts.title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-F: Chemical Reactions as Energy Sources — p.153

kps.titleHtml: KP1: Chemical reactions as energy sources
kps.title: KP1: Chemical reactions as energy sources
kps.coreHtml: Society uses chemical reactions to produce useful energy for heating, transportation, cooking, and technology.
kps.core: Society uses chemical reactions to produce useful energy for heating, transportation, cooking, and technology.
kps.applicationHtml: Compare fossil fuels and alternative energy sources such as hydrogen fuel cells.
kps.application: Compare fossil fuels and alternative energy sources such as hydrogen fuel cells.

kps.titleHtml: KP2: Balanced equations and energy use
kps.title: KP2: Balanced equations and energy use
kps.coreHtml: Energy-source analysis should include the chemical reaction and balanced chemical equation.
kps.core: Energy-source analysis should include the chemical reaction and balanced chemical equation.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect atoms, bonds broken, bonds formed, and energy output in an energy-producing reaction.
kps.application: Connect atoms, bonds broken, bonds formed, and energy output in an energy-producing reaction.

kps.title: KP3: Advantages and disadvantages
kps.coreHtml: Energy sources have environmental, social, and economic benefits and drawbacks.
kps.core: Energy sources have environmental, social, and economic benefits and drawbacks.
kps.applicationHtml: Evaluate an energy source by considering both energy usefulness and broader impacts.
kps.application: Evaluate an energy source by considering both energy usefulness and broader impacts.

chapters.titleHtml: Chapter 2.4: How do atoms rearrange in different types of chemical reactions?
chapters.title: Chapter 2.4: How do atoms rearrange in different types of chemical reactions?

concepts.titleHtml: 2.4 Concept 1: A compound forms in a synthesis reaction and breaks down in a decomposition reaction — p.156–159
concepts.title: 2.4 Concept 1: A compound forms in a synthesis reaction and breaks down in a decomposition reaction — p.156–159

kps.title: KP1: Synthesis reaction
kps.coreHtml: A synthesis reaction is a chemical reaction in which two or more reactants combine to produce a single product.
kps.core: A synthesis reaction is a chemical reaction in which two or more reactants combine to produce a single product.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify synthesis reactions by looking for several reactants forming one compound.
kps.application: Identify synthesis reactions by looking for several reactants forming one compound.

kps.title: KP2: General synthesis pattern
kps.coreHtml: General pattern: \(A + B \rightarrow AB\).
kps.core: General pattern: \(A + B AB\).
kps.applicationHtml: Use the pattern to classify reactions such as metal + non-metal forming an ionic compound.
kps.application: Use the pattern to classify reactions such as metal + non-metal forming an ionic compound.

kps.title: KP3: Synthesis and energy release
kps.coreHtml: Many synthesis reactions are exothermic because bond formation releases energy overall.
kps.core: Many synthesis reactions are exothermic because bond formation releases energy overall.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect heat, light, or other energy release to product formation.
kps.application: Connect heat, light, or other energy release to product formation.

kps.titleHtml: KP4: Synthesis in the environment and technology
kps.title: KP4: Synthesis in the environment and technology
kps.coreHtml: Synthesis reactions occur in processes such as acid precipitation, rust formation, and hydrogen fuel cells.
kps.core: Synthesis reactions occur in processes such as acid precipitation, rust formation, and hydrogen fuel cells.
kps.applicationHtml: Recognize synthesis reactions in environmental and technological contexts.
kps.application: Recognize synthesis reactions in environmental and technological contexts.

kps.title: KP5: Decomposition reaction
kps.coreHtml: A decomposition reaction is a chemical reaction in which one compound breaks down into elements or simpler compounds.
kps.core: A decomposition reaction is a chemical reaction in which one compound breaks down into elements or simpler compounds.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify decomposition reactions by looking for one reactant forming two or more products.
kps.application: Identify decomposition reactions by looking for one reactant forming two or more products.

kps.title: KP6: General decomposition pattern
kps.coreHtml: General pattern: \(AB \rightarrow A + B\).
kps.core: General pattern: \(AB A + B\).
kps.applicationHtml: Treat decomposition as the reverse pattern of synthesis.
kps.application: Treat decomposition as the reverse pattern of synthesis.

kps.title: KP7: Decomposition and energy input
kps.coreHtml: Most decomposition reactions are endothermic because the reactant must absorb enough energy for bonds to break.
kps.core: Most decomposition reactions are endothermic because the reactant must absorb enough energy for bonds to break.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why heat or electricity may be needed to break a compound apart.
kps.application: Explain why heat or electricity may be needed to break a compound apart.

concepts.titleHtml: 2.4 Concept 2: In replacement reactions, elements replace other elements — p.160–164
concepts.title: 2.4 Concept 2: In replacement reactions, elements replace other elements — p.160–164

kps.title: KP1: Single replacement reaction
kps.coreHtml: A single replacement reaction occurs when an element and a compound react to produce another element and another compound.
kps.core: A single replacement reaction occurs when an element and a compound react to produce another element and another compound.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify reactions where one element takes the place of another element in a compound.
kps.application: Identify reactions where one element takes the place of another element in a compound.

kps.title: KP2: Metal replacement pattern
kps.coreHtml: General pattern: \(A + BX \rightarrow AX + B\).
kps.core: General pattern: \(A + BX AX + B\).
kps.applicationHtml: Recognize when a metal element replaces a metal ion in an aqueous compound.
kps.application: Recognize when a metal element replaces a metal ion in an aqueous compound.

kps.title: KP3: Non-metal replacement pattern
kps.coreHtml: General pattern: \(AX + Y \rightarrow AY + X\).
kps.core: General pattern: \(AX + Y AY + X\).
kps.applicationHtml: Recognize when a non-metal element replaces a non-metal ion in a compound.
kps.application: Recognize when a non-metal element replaces a non-metal ion in a compound.

kps.titleHtml: KP4: Hydrogen as a replaceable element
kps.title: KP4: Hydrogen as a replaceable element
kps.coreHtml: In some single replacement reactions, hydrogen in water can behave like a metal.
kps.core: In some single replacement reactions, hydrogen in water can behave like a metal.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how a metal reacting with water can produce hydrogen gas.
kps.application: Explain how a metal reacting with water can produce hydrogen gas.

kps.titleHtml: KP5: Applications of single replacement reactions
kps.title: KP5: Applications of single replacement reactions
kps.coreHtml: Some single replacement reactions release large amounts of energy or produce useful elements.
kps.core: Some single replacement reactions release large amounts of energy or produce useful elements.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect single replacement reactions to bromine production, welding railroad tracks, or joining electrical conductors.
kps.application: Connect single replacement reactions to bromine production, welding railroad tracks, or joining electrical conductors.

kps.title: KP6: Double replacement reaction
kps.coreHtml: A double replacement reaction occurs when the positive ions of two ionic compounds change places to form two new ionic compounds.
kps.core: A double replacement reaction occurs when the positive ions of two ionic compounds change places to form two new ionic compounds.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify reactions where ions exchange partners in aqueous solution.
kps.application: Identify reactions where ions exchange partners in aqueous solution.

kps.title: KP7: Precipitate formation
kps.coreHtml: A precipitate is a solid that forms from a reaction between aqueous solutions.
kps.core: A precipitate is a solid that forms from a reaction between aqueous solutions.
kps.applicationHtml: Use formation of a solid as evidence for some double replacement reactions.
kps.application: Use formation of a solid as evidence for some double replacement reactions.

concepts.titleHtml: 2.4 Concept 3: Most combustion reactions release heat and light — p.165–167
concepts.title: 2.4 Concept 3: Most combustion reactions release heat and light — p.165–167

kps.title: KP1: Combustion reaction
kps.coreHtml: A combustion reaction is a chemical reaction in which an element reacts with oxygen to produce an oxide and heat.
kps.core: A combustion reaction is a chemical reaction in which an element reacts with oxygen to produce an oxide and heat.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify combustion by the involvement of oxygen and energy release.
kps.application: Identify combustion by the involvement of oxygen and energy release.

kps.title: KP2: Hydrocarbon combustion
kps.coreHtml: Hydrocarbons contain carbon and hydrogen; complete combustion produces carbon dioxide and water.
kps.core: Hydrocarbons contain carbon and hydrogen; complete combustion produces carbon dioxide and water.
kps.applicationHtml: Classify burning fuels such as methane, gasoline, and propane as combustion reactions.
kps.application: Classify burning fuels such as methane, gasoline, and propane as combustion reactions.

kps.title: KP3: General hydrocarbon combustion pattern
kps.coreHtml: General pattern: \(C_xH_y + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O\).
kps.core: General pattern: \(C xH y + O 2 CO 2 + H 2O\).
kps.applicationHtml: Predict the common products of complete hydrocarbon combustion.
kps.application: Predict the common products of complete hydrocarbon combustion.

kps.titleHtml: KP4: Combustion as an exothermic process
kps.title: KP4: Combustion as an exothermic process
kps.coreHtml: Most combustion reactions release heat and light.
kps.core: Most combustion reactions release heat and light.
kps.applicationHtml: Use flame, heat, and light as evidence of energy release.
kps.application: Use flame, heat, and light as evidence of energy release.

kps.title: KP5: Combustion and pollution
kps.coreHtml: Combustion of sulfur or nitrogen-containing substances can produce pollutants such as sulfur dioxide or nitrogen dioxide.
kps.core: Combustion of sulfur or nitrogen-containing substances can produce pollutants such as sulfur dioxide or nitrogen dioxide.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect combustion products to air pollution and later acid-forming reactions.
kps.application: Connect combustion products to air pollution and later acid-forming reactions.

kps.title: KP6: Incomplete combustion
kps.coreHtml: Incomplete combustion occurs when oxygen supply is too low and can produce carbon monoxide and soot.
kps.core: Incomplete combustion occurs when oxygen supply is too low and can produce carbon monoxide and soot.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why burning fuels indoors or in poorly ventilated spaces can be dangerous.
kps.application: Explain why burning fuels indoors or in poorly ventilated spaces can be dangerous.

kps.title: KP7: Carbon monoxide hazard
kps.coreHtml: Carbon monoxide is a colourless, odourless, highly toxic gas.
kps.core: Carbon monoxide is a colourless, odourless, highly toxic gas.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect incomplete combustion to the need for carbon monoxide detectors.
kps.application: Connect incomplete combustion to the need for carbon monoxide detectors.

concepts.titleHtml: 2.4 Concept 4: In a neutralization reaction, an acid reacts with a base — p.168–174
concepts.title: 2.4 Concept 4: In a neutralization reaction, an acid reacts with a base — p.168–174

kps.title: KP1: Acid
kps.coreHtml: An acid is a compound that forms \(H^+\) ions when dissolved in water.
kps.core: An acid is a compound that forms \(H +\) ions when dissolved in water.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify acidic solutions from formulas, properties, or ion formation.
kps.application: Identify acidic solutions from formulas, properties, or ion formation.

kps.title: KP2: Base
kps.coreHtml: A base is a compound that forms \(OH^-\) ions when dissolved in water.
kps.core: A base is a compound that forms \(OH -\) ions when dissolved in water.
kps.applicationHtml: Identify basic solutions from formulas, properties, or ion formation.
kps.application: Identify basic solutions from formulas, properties, or ion formation.

kps.title: KP3: Acid-base indicators
kps.coreHtml: An acid-base indicator changes colour in response to whether a solution is acidic or basic.
kps.core: An acid-base indicator changes colour in response to whether a solution is acidic or basic.
kps.applicationHtml: Use litmus or other indicators to classify unknown solutions.
kps.application: Use litmus or other indicators to classify unknown solutions.

kps.title: KP4: pH scale
kps.coreHtml: The pH scale ranges from 0 to 14 and measures acidity or basicity.
kps.core: The pH scale ranges from 0 to 14 and measures acidity or basicity.
kps.applicationHtml: Classify solutions as acidic \((pH < 7)\), neutral \((pH = 7)\), or basic \((pH > 7)\).
kps.application: Classify solutions as acidic \((pH 7)\).

kps.title: KP5: pH and ion concentration
kps.coreHtml: Acids have more \(H^+\) than \(OH^-\); bases have more \(OH^-\) than \(H^+\); neutral solutions have equal amounts.
kps.core: Acids have more \(H +\) than \(OH -\); bases have more \(OH -\) than \(H +\); neutral solutions have equal amounts.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect pH values to relative hydrogen-ion and hydroxide-ion concentrations.
kps.application: Connect pH values to relative hydrogen-ion and hydroxide-ion concentrations.

kps.title: KP6: Neutralization reaction
kps.coreHtml: A neutralization reaction occurs when an acid reacts with a base to form water and a salt.
kps.core: A neutralization reaction occurs when an acid reacts with a base to form water and a salt.
kps.applicationHtml: Recognize neutralization as a common acid-base reaction in labs, medicine, and the environment.
kps.application: Recognize neutralization as a common acid-base reaction in labs, medicine, and the environment.

kps.title: KP7: General neutralization pattern
kps.coreHtml: General pattern: \(HA + BOH \rightarrow BA + HOH\), or acid + base \(\rightarrow\) salt + water.
kps.core: General pattern: \(HA + BOH BA + HOH\), or acid + base \( \) salt + water.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain why neutralization is a type of double replacement reaction.
kps.application: Explain why neutralization is a type of double replacement reaction.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Extension — At Issue: What are the consequences of increasing ocean acidity? — p.176–177
concepts.title: Optional Extension — At Issue: What are the consequences of increasing ocean acidity? — p.176–177

kps.titleHtml: KP1: Fossil fuels and carbon dioxide
kps.title: KP1: Fossil fuels and carbon dioxide
kps.coreHtml: Burning fossil fuels releases carbon dioxide into the atmosphere.
kps.core: Burning fossil fuels releases carbon dioxide into the atmosphere.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect combustion to increasing atmospheric \(CO_2\).
kps.application: Connect combustion to increasing atmospheric \(CO 2\).

kps.titleHtml: KP2: Carbon dioxide and ocean acidity
kps.title: KP2: Carbon dioxide and ocean acidity
kps.coreHtml: \(CO_2\) dissolves in ocean water and can form carbonic acid.
kps.core: \(CO 2\) dissolves in ocean water and can form carbonic acid.
kps.applicationHtml: Explain how atmospheric \(CO_2\) affects ocean chemistry.
kps.application: Explain how atmospheric \(CO 2\) affects ocean chemistry.

kps.title: KP3: Ocean organisms and shells
kps.coreHtml: Increased acidity can affect organisms that build shells or skeletons from carbonate materials.
kps.core: Increased acidity can affect organisms that build shells or skeletons from carbonate materials.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect ocean acidification to food webs and human communities that depend on ocean organisms.
kps.application: Connect ocean acidification to food webs and human communities that depend on ocean organisms.

kps.title: KP4: Chemistry and global consequences
kps.coreHtml: Chemical changes in the ocean can affect ecosystems, food supply, and livelihoods.
kps.core: Chemical changes in the ocean can affect ecosystems, food supply, and livelihoods.
kps.applicationHtml: Evaluate ocean acidity as both a chemistry issue and a social-environmental issue.
kps.application: Evaluate ocean acidity as both a chemistry issue and a social-environmental issue.

kps.titleHtml: KP1: Properties of acids and bases
kps.title: KP1: Properties of acids and bases
kps.coreHtml: Acids and bases can be identified using observable properties and tests.
kps.core: Acids and bases can be identified using observable properties and tests.
kps.applicationHtml: Use evidence such as indicator colour, pH, or reaction behaviour to classify unknowns.
kps.application: Use evidence such as indicator colour, pH, or reaction behaviour to classify unknowns.

kps.title: KP2: Testing unknown solutions
kps.coreHtml: Unknown substances can be identified by comparing test results with known acid-base properties.
kps.core: Unknown substances can be identified by comparing test results with known acid-base properties.
kps.applicationHtml: Design a safe procedure for identifying unknown acidic, basic, or neutral substances.
kps.application: Design a safe procedure for identifying unknown acidic, basic, or neutral substances.

kps.title: KP3: Evidence-based identification
kps.coreHtml: Reliable identification requires organized observations and comparison across multiple tests.
kps.core: Reliable identification requires organized observations and comparison across multiple tests.
kps.applicationHtml: Support a claim about an unknown substance using recorded evidence.
kps.application: Support a claim about an unknown substance using recorded evidence.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-H: Six Types of Reactions — p.180–182
concepts.title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-H: Six Types of Reactions — p.180–182

kps.title: KP1: Reaction classification
kps.coreHtml: Chemical reactions can be classified as synthesis, decomposition, single replacement, double replacement, combustion, or neutralization.
kps.core: Chemical reactions can be classified as synthesis, decomposition, single replacement, double replacement, combustion, or neutralization.
kps.applicationHtml: Sort reactions by reactant-product patterns.
kps.application: Sort reactions by reactant-product patterns.

kps.title: KP2: Predicting products
kps.coreHtml: Reaction type can help predict likely products.
kps.core: Reaction type can help predict likely products.
kps.applicationHtml: Use patterns to predict products before balancing the equation.
kps.application: Use patterns to predict products before balancing the equation.

kps.title: KP3: Balanced equations
kps.coreHtml: Reaction classification and product prediction must still follow conservation of mass.
kps.core: Reaction classification and product prediction must still follow conservation of mass.
kps.applicationHtml: Balance predicted chemical equations using coefficients.
kps.application: Balance predicted chemical equations using coefficients.

kps.title: KP4: Observation and reaction type
kps.coreHtml: Visible evidence such as gas formation, precipitate formation, heat, light, or colour change can support reaction classification.
kps.core: Visible evidence such as gas formation, precipitate formation, heat, light, or colour change can support reaction classification.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect lab observations to reaction-type reasoning.
kps.application: Connect lab observations to reaction-type reasoning.

concepts.titleHtml: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-I: Evaluate the Effectiveness of Antacids — p.183
concepts.title: Optional Investigation — Investigation 2-I: Evaluate the Effectiveness of Antacids — p.183

kps.title: KP1: Antacids and neutralization
kps.coreHtml: Antacids work by neutralizing stomach acid.
kps.core: Antacids work by neutralizing stomach acid.
kps.applicationHtml: Connect acid-base reactions to medicine and digestion.
kps.application: Connect acid-base reactions to medicine and digestion.

kps.title: KP2: Experimental variables
kps.coreHtml: A fair antacid test needs one independent variable, one dependent variable, and controlled conditions.
kps.core: A fair antacid test needs one independent variable, one dependent variable, and controlled conditions.
kps.applicationHtml: Design an experiment that compares antacids using consistent criteria.
kps.application: Design an experiment that compares antacids using consistent criteria.

kps.title: KP3: Effectiveness criteria
kps.coreHtml: Antacid effectiveness may depend on amount needed, cost, side effects, and neutralizing ability.
kps.core: Antacid effectiveness may depend on amount needed, cost, side effects, and neutralizing ability.
kps.applicationHtml: Choose an antacid recommendation based on evidence rather than one measurement alone.
kps.application: Choose an antacid recommendation based on evidence rather than one measurement alone.

kps.title: KP4: Balanced neutralization equations
kps.coreHtml: Each antacid reaction can be represented with a balanced neutralization equation.
kps.core: Each antacid reaction can be represented with a balanced neutralization equation.
kps.applicationHtml: Link experimental results to the chemical reaction that neutralizes acid.
kps.application: Link experimental results to the chemical reaction that neutralizes acid.

summaryHtml:
Unit Summary

- Total Chapters: 4

- Total Core Sections: 12

- Total Core Knowledge Points: 82

- Optional Extension / Investigation Sections: 18

- Optional Extension / Investigation Knowledge Points: 62

- Top 5 Core Formulas:

• Synthesis reaction pattern: \(A + B \rightarrow AB\)

• Decomposition reaction pattern: \(AB \rightarrow A + B\)

• Hydrocarbon combustion pattern: \(C_xH_y + O_2 \rightarrow CO_2 + H_2O\)

• Neutralization pattern: acid + base \(\rightarrow\) salt + water

• Conservation of mass in balanced equations: atoms of each element on reactant side = atoms of each element on product side

- Top 5 Common Error Areas:

• Confusing ionic compounds, covalent compounds, molecules, and formula units.

• Changing subscripts instead of coefficients when balancing equations.

• Confusing activation energy with overall energy change.

• Confusing single replacement and double replacement reactions.

• Treating every combustion reaction as complete combustion and forgetting carbon monoxide risk.

- Suggested Learning Order:

• Start with chemistry in everyday life and safety: chemical reactions, WHMIS, SDS, protective equipment, and environmental risk.

• Study bonding and reaction foundations: ionic compounds, covalent compounds, molecules, formula units, bond breaking, bond forming, and conservation of mass.

• Practise chemical equations: reactants, products, word equations, skeleton equations, coefficients, subscripts, states of matter, and balancing.

• Learn energy in reactions: activation energy, system and surroundings, exothermic reactions, endothermic reactions, and energy-level diagrams.

• Finish with reaction types: synthesis, decomposition, single replacement, double replacement, combustion, acids, bases, pH, and neutralization.

- Suggested Review Order:

• Review safety and chemical vocabulary first: chemical reaction, WHMIS, SDS, system, surroundings, reactant, product.

• Review bonding differences: ionic bonding, covalent bonding, molecules, formula units, and valence electrons.

• Practise balancing equations and explaining conservation of mass.

• Compare exothermic and endothermic reactions using energy transfer and temperature evidence.

• Sort reaction examples by pattern: synthesis, decomposition, replacement, combustion, and neutralization.

• Review common hazards: pollutants, incomplete combustion, carbon monoxide, acids, bases, and chemical waste.
